metadata.title: AAO Preferred Practice Patterns - Interactive Study Guide
metadata.source: American Academy of Ophthalmology Preferred Practice Patterns

ppps.title: Primary Open-Angle Glaucoma (POAG)

keyRecommendations.text: Perform gonioscopy on all glaucoma suspects and patients to classify type of glaucoma
Assess optic nerve head and RNFL with both clinical examination and imaging (OCT)
Set target IOP based on the extent of optic nerve damage and other risk factors; generally 25% reduction from baseline for early glaucoma
Medical therapy (prostaglandin analogs) is the most common initial treatment
Laser trabeculoplasty (SLT) may be used as initial therapy — LiGHT trial showed non-inferiority to drops
Consider surgical intervention when medical and laser therapy fail to achieve target IOP or disease progresses
Measure central corneal thickness as it affects IOP measurement accuracy (thin corneas underestimate, thick overestimate)
Perform visual field testing (standard automated perimetry, 24-2 or 30-2) at baseline and periodically

diagnosticCriteria: Open angles on gonioscopy
Characteristic optic nerve damage (increased C/D ratio, notching, disc hemorrhage)
Corresponding visual field loss (arcuate scotoma, nasal step, paracentral defects)
IOP may or may not be elevated (normal tension glaucoma exists)
Absence of secondary causes

managementAlgorithm.action: Establish baseline: IOP (multiple readings), CCT, gonioscopy, optic nerve assessment, OCT RNFL/GCC, visual fields (24-2)
Set target IOP: 25% reduction for early, 30-40% for moderate, >40% for severe glaucoma
First-line treatment: prostaglandin analog (latanoprost, bimatoprost, travoprost) OR SLT
If inadequate response: add second agent (beta-blocker timolol, alpha-agonist brimonidine, or CAI dorzolamide)
If maximum medical therapy insufficient: consider laser (SLT if not already done, or cyclophotocoagulation)
Surgical options: trabeculectomy, tube shunt (Ahmed, Baerveldt), MIGS (iStent, Hydrus, XEN gel stent, Kahook dual blade)
Follow-up: every 3-6 months for stable disease, more frequently after treatment changes

ppps.followUp: Every 3-12 months depending on severity and stability. More frequent early after diagnosis or treatment change. Annual visual fields and OCT minimum.
boardPearls: OHTS trial: treatment of ocular hypertension reduces risk of developing POAG by ~50%
AGIS, CIGTS, EMGT — all landmark trials showing IOP lowering prevents progression
LiGHT trial: SLT as first-line was non-inferior to drops and more cost-effective at 3 years
Target IOP is not a fixed number — it must be individualized and reassessed
Prostaglandin analogs are first-line because of once-daily dosing, efficacy (25-33% IOP reduction), and minimal systemic side effects
Beta-blockers contraindicated in asthma, COPD, bradycardia, heart block
Brimonidine: avoid in children <2 years (CNS depression risk)
Disc hemorrhage is a sign of progression — lower the target IOP
pitfalls: Forgetting to measure CCT — thin corneas underestimate true IOP
Not performing gonioscopy — misclassifying angle-closure as open-angle
Using IOP alone to diagnose glaucoma — normal tension glaucoma exists
Not setting a specific target IOP — saying 'lower the pressure' is insufficient
Prescribing beta-blockers without checking for asthma/COPD/cardiac history

quizQuestions.question: What is the first-line medical therapy for POAG per AAO PPP?
quizQuestions.answer: Prostaglandin analog (latanoprost, bimatoprost, travoprost, tafluprost)

quizQuestions.question: Per the LiGHT trial, what was shown about SLT as initial therapy?
quizQuestions.answer: SLT was non-inferior to medical therapy and more cost-effective at 3 years
distractors: SLT was inferior to medical therapy
SLT only works in pseudophakic patients

quizQuestions.question: What target IOP reduction is recommended for early POAG?
quizQuestions.answer: 25% reduction from baseline

quizQuestions.question: Which landmark trial showed that treating ocular hypertension reduces POAG risk by ~50%?
quizQuestions.answer: Ocular Hypertension Treatment Study (OHTS)

ppps.title: Primary Open-Angle Glaucoma Suspect

keyRecommendations.text: Define POAG suspect as having one or more risk factors: elevated IOP, suspicious optic disc, family history, thin CCT
Baseline evaluation includes IOP, CCT, gonioscopy, dilated fundus exam, OCT RNFL, and visual fields
Treatment is NOT required for all suspects — individualize based on risk assessment
Re-evaluate every 6-24 months depending on risk level

diagnosticCriteria: IOP > 21 mmHg without optic nerve damage
Suspicious optic nerve appearance without definitive damage
Strong family history of glaucoma
Thin central corneal thickness (<555 microns)
No visual field loss attributable to glaucoma

managementAlgorithm.action: Establish baseline: IOP (multiple readings at different times), CCT, gonioscopy, optic nerve assessment, OCT, visual fields
Risk stratify: use OHTS risk calculator — age, IOP, CCT, cup-to-disc ratio, pattern standard deviation
Low risk (<5%/5yr): observe every 12-24 months
Moderate risk (5-15%/5yr): observe every 6-12 months, consider treatment if progression detected
High risk (>15%/5yr): strongly consider initiating treatment

ppps.followUp: Every 6-24 months based on risk stratification. Serial OCT and visual fields to detect conversion.
boardPearls: Not all ocular hypertension needs treatment — risk stratification is key
OHTS showed that only ~10% of untreated OHT patients develop glaucoma at 5 years
CCT is a powerful independent risk factor — thin corneas = higher risk even at 'normal' IOP
Disc asymmetry >0.2 C/D ratio between eyes is suspicious for glaucoma
Pattern standard deviation (PSD) on visual fields is an early indicator of localized loss
pitfalls: Treating every patient with IOP > 21 — many never develop glaucoma
Not measuring CCT — critical for risk assessment
Using a single IOP reading — IOP fluctuates diurnally
Not getting baseline visual fields and OCT — you need them to detect progression later

quizQuestions.question: What percentage of untreated OHT patients develop POAG at 5 years per OHTS?
quizQuestions.answer: Approximately 10%

quizQuestions.question: Which CCT value is considered a risk factor for glaucoma development?
quizQuestions.answer: < 555 microns (thin cornea)
distractors: CCT is not a risk factor

ppps.title: Primary Angle-Closure Disease

keyRecommendations.text: Perform gonioscopy on all patients with elevated IOP or narrow anterior chambers
Laser peripheral iridotomy (LPI) is the initial treatment for primary angle closure (PAC) and acute angle-closure crisis
ALWAYS treat the fellow eye prophylactically with LPI in acute angle closure
In acute crisis: lower IOP medically first (timolol, brimonidine, pilocarpine 1-2%, acetazolamide, mannitol if needed)
Lens extraction (clear or cataractous) is increasingly recognized as a definitive treatment for angle closure — EAGLE study

diagnosticCriteria: Primary angle-closure suspect (PACS): ≥180 degrees of iridotrabecular contact, no PAS, normal IOP, no optic neuropathy
Primary angle closure (PAC): iridotrabecular contact with elevated IOP or PAS, no optic neuropathy
Primary angle-closure glaucoma (PACG): angle closure with glaucomatous optic neuropathy
Acute angle-closure crisis: sudden IOP elevation with symptoms (pain, nausea, blurred vision, halos)

managementAlgorithm.action: ACUTE CRISIS: Timolol 0.5% + Brimonidine 0.2% + Pilocarpine 1-2% + Acetazolamide 500mg IV/PO + Mannitol 20% IV if needed
Perform LPI when cornea clears (may need glycerin to clear corneal edema)
ALWAYS perform prophylactic LPI on the fellow eye
Post-LPI: check IOP, gonioscopy to confirm angle opened
If angle remains narrow post-LPI (plateau iris): consider argon laser peripheral iridoplasty
Consider lens extraction if lens is a major component of the mechanism (EAGLE study supports this)

ppps.followUp: 24-48 hours after acute episode, then 1 week, 1 month, then every 3-6 months. Long-term monitoring for chronic angle closure development.
boardPearls: EAGLE study: clear lens extraction was superior to LPI for controlling IOP in PACG
Acute angle closure is a TRUE EMERGENCY — delay can cause permanent vision loss
Fellow eye prophylactic LPI — forgetting this is a FATAL FLAW on boards
Pilocarpine may NOT work until IOP is lowered (sphincter ischemia at very high IOP)
Plateau iris: suspect if angle remains narrow after LPI — diagnosed with UBM or anterior segment OCT
Risk factors for angle closure: hyperopia, shallow AC, thick/anteriorly positioned lens, Asian descent, female, older age
pitfalls: Not treating the fellow eye with prophylactic LPI — this is a board exam fatal flaw
Giving pilocarpine first without lowering IOP — it won't work if IOP is very high
Not doing gonioscopy before and after LPI to confirm mechanism
Forgetting to consider plateau iris if angle stays narrow after LPI
Using mydriatics in a patient with narrow angles without doing LPI first

quizQuestions.question: What is the FIRST medication to give in acute angle-closure crisis?
quizQuestions.answer: Timolol 0.5% + Brimonidine 0.2% (aqueous suppressants first, then pilocarpine when IOP starts to decrease)

quizQuestions.question: What did the EAGLE study demonstrate?
quizQuestions.answer: Clear lens extraction was superior to LPI for IOP control in primary angle-closure disease
distractors: SLT is better than LPI for angle closure
LPI prevents all angle closure attacks
Argon laser is superior to Nd:YAG for LPI

quizQuestions.question: What must ALWAYS be done for the fellow eye in acute angle closure?
quizQuestions.answer: Prophylactic laser peripheral iridotomy (LPI)

ppps.title: Age-Related Macular Degeneration (AMD)

keyRecommendations.text: Recommend AREDS2 supplements for intermediate AMD or advanced AMD in one eye
Anti-VEGF therapy is first-line for neovascular (wet) AMD
Intravitreal injections of anti-VEGF agents should be initiated promptly upon diagnosis of wet AMD
Regular monitoring with OCT to guide treatment decisions in wet AMD
Complement inhibitors (pegcetacoplan, avacincaptad pegol) are now approved for geographic atrophy
Counsel patients about Amsler grid monitoring for early detection of conversion

diagnosticCriteria: Early AMD: medium drusen (63-125 microns), no pigmentary changes
Intermediate AMD: large drusen (>125 microns) or pigmentary changes
Advanced dry AMD (geographic atrophy): well-defined areas of RPE/photoreceptor atrophy
Neovascular AMD: choroidal neovascularization (CNV) with fluid, hemorrhage, or exudate

managementAlgorithm.action: Classify AMD stage: early, intermediate, or advanced (dry vs wet)
Early AMD: observe, lifestyle counseling (stop smoking, healthy diet, UV protection)
Intermediate AMD: AREDS2 supplements (Vit C 500mg, Vit E 400IU, Zinc 80mg, Lutein 10mg, Zeaxanthin 2mg) — NO beta-carotene
Wet AMD: intravitreal anti-VEGF injection (bevacizumab, ranibizumab, aflibercept, faricimab) — initiate promptly
Follow wet AMD with monthly OCT; treat-and-extend or PRN dosing after loading doses
Geographic atrophy: complement inhibitors (pegcetacoplan monthly, avacincaptad pegol monthly) to slow progression
Low vision rehabilitation referral for advanced cases

ppps.followUp: Wet AMD: monthly during loading phase, then per treat-and-extend protocol. Dry AMD: every 6-24 months depending on severity. Amsler grid daily.
boardPearls: CATT trial: bevacizumab was non-inferior to ranibizumab for wet AMD — key for cost discussions
AREDS2 replaced beta-carotene with lutein/zeaxanthin — beta-carotene increases lung cancer risk in smokers
Faricimab is bispecific (anti-VEGF-A + anti-Ang-2) — allows longer treatment intervals (up to 16 weeks)
Geographic atrophy can now be treated — pegcetacoplan (C3 inhibitor) and avacincaptad pegol (C5 inhibitor)
Know the drusen size classification: small <63 microns, medium 63-125, large >125
OCT findings in wet AMD: subretinal fluid, intraretinal fluid, PED, subretinal hyperreflective material
Anti-VEGF agents do NOT cure wet AMD — lifelong monitoring and treatment required
pitfalls: Prescribing beta-carotene in AREDS2 formula — replaced with lutein/zeaxanthin due to lung cancer risk
Delaying anti-VEGF treatment in wet AMD — prompt treatment preserves vision
Not counseling about Amsler grid for home monitoring
Forgetting that bevacizumab is off-label but effective and much cheaper
Not knowing the new GA treatments (complement inhibitors)

quizQuestions.question: What AREDS2 supplement replaced beta-carotene and why?
quizQuestions.answer: Lutein (10mg) and Zeaxanthin (2mg) replaced beta-carotene because beta-carotene increases lung cancer risk in smokers
distractors: Vitamin A because of liver toxicity

quizQuestions.question: What did the CATT trial demonstrate?
quizQuestions.answer: Bevacizumab was non-inferior to ranibizumab for wet AMD treatment

quizQuestions.question: What is the mechanism of faricimab?
quizQuestions.answer: Bispecific antibody targeting both VEGF-A and Angiopoietin-2 (Ang-2)

ppps.title: Diabetic Retinopathy

keyRecommendations.text: Screen type 1 DM patients within 5 years of diagnosis; type 2 DM at time of diagnosis
Classify DR severity using ETDRS scale: mild/moderate/severe NPDR, PDR
Anti-VEGF is first-line for center-involving diabetic macular edema (DME)
PRP remains standard of care for high-risk PDR, though anti-VEGF (Protocol S) is an alternative
Optimize systemic control: HbA1c, blood pressure, lipids — co-manage with PCP/endocrinology

diagnosticCriteria: Mild NPDR: microaneurysms only
Moderate NPDR: microaneurysms + dot/blot hemorrhages, hard exudates, cotton-wool spots
Severe NPDR (4-2-1 rule): hemorrhages in 4 quadrants OR venous beading in 2+ quadrants OR IRMA in 1+ quadrant
PDR: neovascularization of disc (NVD) or elsewhere (NVE), vitreous/preretinal hemorrhage
DME: retinal thickening within 1 disc diameter of foveal center (center-involving vs non-center-involving)

managementAlgorithm.action: Classify DR severity and determine if DME is present
Mild/moderate NPDR without DME: observation, optimize systemic control, follow every 6-12 months
Severe NPDR: close follow-up every 2-4 months, consider early PRP if high risk of progression or poor follow-up
PDR: PRP (panretinal photocoagulation) OR anti-VEGF therapy (Protocol S showed non-inferiority of ranibizumab to PRP at 2 years)
Center-involving DME: anti-VEGF injection (Protocol T: aflibercept for VA ≤20/50; all agents equivalent for VA ≥20/40)
Non-center-involving DME: observation or focal/grid laser if progression or threatening fovea
Vitrectomy for: non-clearing vitreous hemorrhage (1-3 months), tractional retinal detachment involving macula

ppps.followUp: Mild NPDR: 12 months. Moderate NPDR: 6-12 months. Severe NPDR: 2-4 months. PDR: 2-4 months. DME on treatment: monthly during loading phase.
boardPearls: 4-2-1 rule for severe NPDR: 4 quadrants of hemorrhages, 2+ quadrants venous beading, 1+ quadrant IRMA — ANY ONE qualifies
DRCR.net Protocol T: aflibercept > bevacizumab/ranibizumab for DME with VA ≤20/50 at year 1
Protocol S: anti-VEGF (ranibizumab) was non-inferior to PRP for PDR at 2 years with better visual outcomes
DRS/ETDRS: PRP reduces severe vision loss by ~50% in high-risk PDR
Pregnancy can accelerate diabetic retinopathy — screen each trimester
Rapid improvement in glycemic control can temporarily worsen DR — 'early worsening'
Rubeosis iridis (NVI) indicates ischemia — may need PRP urgently to prevent NVG
pitfalls: Not knowing the 4-2-1 rule for severe NPDR classification
Using focal laser as first-line for center-involving DME instead of anti-VEGF
Not screening type 2 DM at diagnosis — many already have retinopathy
Forgetting to mention systemic optimization (HbA1c, BP, lipids)
Not warning pregnant patients with DM about worsening retinopathy risk

quizQuestions.question: What defines severe NPDR using the 4-2-1 rule?
quizQuestions.answer: Hemorrhages in 4 quadrants OR venous beading in 2+ quadrants OR IRMA in 1+ quadrant — ANY ONE qualifies

quizQuestions.question: Per DRCR.net Protocol T, which anti-VEGF is best for DME with VA 20/50 or worse?
quizQuestions.answer: Aflibercept (superior at 1 year for VA ≤20/50)
distractors: All agents are equally effective at all VA levels
Bevacizumab is always the best choice

quizQuestions.question: When should type 2 DM patients first be screened for retinopathy?
quizQuestions.answer: At the time of diagnosis of diabetes

ppps.title: Retinal Vein Occlusions (RVO)

keyRecommendations.text: Anti-VEGF is first-line treatment for macular edema from CRVO and BRVO
Monitor for neovascularization, especially NVI/NVA after CRVO (neovascular glaucoma risk)
Systemic workup: blood pressure, blood glucose, CBC, lipid panel; hypercoagulability workup for young patients or bilateral RVO
PRP for neovascularization secondary to ischemic CRVO

diagnosticCriteria: BRVO: flame-shaped hemorrhages in one sector of retina following venous distribution
CRVO: diffuse retinal hemorrhages in all quadrants, dilated tortuous veins, disc edema, cotton wool spots
Ischemic CRVO: >10 disc areas of non-perfusion on FFA, VA typically <20/200, RAPD present

managementAlgorithm.action: Confirm diagnosis, determine type (BRVO vs CRVO), assess for ischemia (FFA if needed)
Systemic workup: BP, glucose, CBC, lipids; hypercoagulability panel if young or bilateral
Macular edema: anti-VEGF injection (BRAVO/CRUISE trials support ranibizumab; also bevacizumab, aflibercept effective)
Alternative for macular edema: dexamethasone intravitreal implant (Ozurdex) especially in pseudophakic patients
Monitor for neovascularization (NVI, NVD, NVE) — PRP if present
Ischemic CRVO: close follow-up every 1-2 months for NVG development, PRP if neovascularization develops
BRVO: if no improvement with anti-VEGF, consider grid laser for persistent macular edema

ppps.followUp: Monthly during active treatment. Every 1-3 months during monitoring phase. Watch for NVG development in ischemic CRVO for at least 6 months.
boardPearls: BRAVO/CRUISE trials: ranibizumab effective for macular edema in BRVO/CRVO
Ischemic CRVO: '90-day glaucoma' — NVG can develop 2-4 months after CRVO
Check for NVI at every visit in CRVO patients — use gonioscopy
Young patients with RVO: consider hypercoagulability workup (protein C, S, antithrombin III, factor V Leiden, antiphospholipid antibodies)
Dexamethasone implant (Ozurdex): good alternative especially in pseudophakic patients (steroid-induced cataract not a concern)
SCORE study: intravitreal triamcinolone for CRVO macular edema was effective but with IOP/cataract risks
pitfalls: Not checking for NVI/NVA in CRVO patients — neovascular glaucoma can develop rapidly
Forgetting systemic workup — RVO has systemic vascular risk factor implications
Not doing hypercoagulability workup in young patients
Using intravitreal steroids in phakic patients without discussing cataract/IOP risks

quizQuestions.question: What is the '90-day glaucoma' associated with ischemic CRVO?
quizQuestions.answer: Neovascular glaucoma (NVG) developing 2-4 months after ischemic CRVO due to rubeosis iridis

quizQuestions.question: What is first-line treatment for macular edema from CRVO?
quizQuestions.answer: Intravitreal anti-VEGF injection

ppps.title: Retinal and Ophthalmic Artery Occlusions

keyRecommendations.text: CRAO is a stroke equivalent — urgent systemic stroke workup required
Immediate interventions (within hours): ocular massage, anterior chamber paracentesis, IOP-lowering agents
Refer for urgent carotid evaluation and cardiac workup
Rule out giant cell arteritis in patients >50 with any arterial occlusion
Intra-arterial thrombolysis may be considered within 4.5 hours (EAGLE study) but remains controversial

diagnosticCriteria: CRAO: sudden painless vision loss, cherry red spot, pale/edematous retina, attenuated arterioles, boxcar segmentation
BRAO: sectoral retinal whitening in distribution of affected arteriole
Ophthalmic artery occlusion: no cherry red spot (choroidal circulation also affected), severe vision loss

managementAlgorithm.action: EMERGENCY: Immediate ocular massage (intermittent digital pressure), AC paracentesis to lower IOP rapidly
Topical IOP-lowering: timolol 0.5%, systemic acetazolamide 500mg IV
Check ESR, CRP, CBC urgently — rule out GCA if age >50
If GCA suspected: start IV methylprednisolone 1g/day x 3 days immediately, temporal artery biopsy within 2 weeks
Urgent stroke workup: carotid Doppler, echocardiogram, cardiac monitoring, brain MRI/CT
Cardiology/neurology co-management for secondary prevention (antiplatelets, statins, BP control)

ppps.followUp: Daily for first week if GCA suspected. Then every 1-4 weeks. Long-term vascular risk management with PCP/cardiology.
boardPearls: CRAO is a stroke equivalent — same urgency as a cerebral stroke
Cherry red spot = fovea appears red because it is supplied by choroid (visible through thin fovea) while surrounding ischemic retina is pale
GCA must be ruled out in ALL patients >50 — start steroids BEFORE biopsy
Arteritic CRAO (GCA) vs non-arteritic: arteritic has worse prognosis, bilateral risk, and systemic threat to life
Window for treatment is very narrow — retinal tolerance time for ischemia is ~90 minutes
Hollenhorst plaque (cholesterol embolus) = carotid disease; calcific plaque = cardiac valve disease
pitfalls: Not checking ESR/CRP in patients >50 — GCA can cause bilateral blindness and systemic complications
Waiting for biopsy results before starting steroids for suspected GCA
Not performing urgent carotid/cardiac workup — CRAO predicts stroke risk
Confusing ophthalmic artery occlusion with CRAO — no cherry red spot in OAO

quizQuestions.question: Why is CRAO considered a 'stroke equivalent'?
quizQuestions.answer: It shares the same vascular risk factors and pathophysiology as cerebral stroke, and patients are at high risk for subsequent stroke
distractors: Because it always occurs with concurrent stroke
Because treatment is the same as stroke
Because it affects the same blood vessels

quizQuestions.question: In suspected GCA with CRAO, when should steroids be started?
quizQuestions.answer: Immediately — BEFORE temporal artery biopsy results are available

ppps.title: PVD, Retinal Breaks, and Lattice Degeneration

keyRecommendations.text: Dilated fundus exam with scleral depression for patients with symptoms of acute PVD (flashes, floaters)
Horseshoe tears with symptoms: treat with laser photocoagulation or cryotherapy
Asymptomatic horseshoe tears: treatment is recommended, especially if fellow eye had RD
Asymptomatic round holes: generally observe unless high-risk features present
Lattice degeneration without symptoms or breaks: observation usually sufficient

diagnosticCriteria: PVD: Weiss ring, pigmented cells (Shafer sign = tobacco dust = concerning for retinal break)
Horseshoe tear: U-shaped retinal break with vitreous traction on flap
Operculated hole: round hole with free-floating operculum (lower risk than horseshoe tear)
Lattice degeneration: circumferentially oriented area of retinal thinning with overlying vitreous liquefaction

managementAlgorithm.action: Symptomatic PVD: dilated exam with scleral depression within 24 hours
If retinal break found: laser photocoagulation (3 rows around break) or cryotherapy
If no break on initial exam: repeat exam in 4-6 weeks (delayed retinal breaks can occur)
Symptomatic horseshoe tear: treat urgently
Asymptomatic horseshoe tear: treat (especially if fellow eye had RD, aphakia/pseudophakia, high myopia)
Lattice degeneration: treat only if symptomatic breaks within it, or prophylactically if fellow eye had RD from lattice

ppps.followUp: After PVD symptoms: recheck in 4-6 weeks, then PRN. After laser treatment: 1-2 weeks to confirm treatment effect.
boardPearls: Shafer sign (tobacco dust/pigmented cells in anterior vitreous) = pathognomonic for retinal break — do NOT dismiss this finding
50% of retinal detachments start from horseshoe tears
Lattice degeneration is present in ~8% of the population — most never develop RD
Fellow eye of RD patient has ~10% risk of developing RD — prophylactic treatment of breaks is warranted
Lightning flashes (photopsia) = vitreous traction on retina — must rule out retinal break
New shower of floaters is more concerning than a single long-standing floater
pitfalls: Not performing scleral depression — peripheral breaks can be missed without it
Not rechecking in 4-6 weeks after negative initial exam for PVD symptoms — delayed breaks occur
Treating all lattice degeneration prophylactically — only treat if breaks present or high-risk features
Missing the Shafer sign — check the anterior vitreous carefully

quizQuestions.question: What is the Shafer sign and what does it indicate?
quizQuestions.answer: Pigmented cells (tobacco dust) in the anterior vitreous — pathognomonic for a retinal break

quizQuestions.question: When should a patient with new PVD symptoms be re-examined if the initial exam is negative?
quizQuestions.answer: In 4-6 weeks (delayed retinal breaks can develop)
distractors: No follow-up needed if initial exam is negative

ppps.title: Blepharitis

keyRecommendations.text: Blepharitis is a chronic condition — counsel patients that it is managed, not cured
Lid hygiene (warm compresses, lid scrubs) is the foundation of treatment
Topical antibiotics (erythromycin ointment, bacitracin) for anterior blepharitis
Oral doxycycline or azithromycin for moderate-severe posterior blepharitis (meibomian gland dysfunction)
Consider Demodex testing if refractory — treat with tea tree oil-based lid scrubs or ivermectin

diagnosticCriteria: Anterior blepharitis: collarettes/scurf on lashes (staphylococcal), greasy scales (seborrheic)
Posterior blepharitis (MGD): thickened/turbid meibomian gland secretions, gland dropout, lid margin telangiectasia
Demodex blepharitis: cylindrical dandruff (collarettes) at base of lashes, refractory to standard treatment

managementAlgorithm.action: Warm compresses BID (5-10 minutes) + lid scrubs (dilute baby shampoo or commercial lid wipes)
Anterior blepharitis: erythromycin or bacitracin ointment to lid margins QHS
Posterior blepharitis/MGD: meibomian gland expression, omega-3 supplements, topical azithromycin
Moderate-severe: oral doxycycline 50-100mg BID (or azithromycin pulsed dosing) for 6-12 weeks
Refractory: consider Demodex (tea tree oil scrubs, ivermectin), thermal pulsation devices (LipiFlow), IPL
Associated dry eye: preservative-free artificial tears QID, topical cyclosporine or lifitegrast

ppps.followUp: 4-6 weeks after initiating treatment. Then every 3-6 months for maintenance. Emphasize chronic nature and maintenance therapy.
boardPearls: Blepharitis is MANAGED, not CURED — always counsel on chronicity
Demodex: look for cylindrical dandruff at lash base — this is the hallmark finding
Doxycycline: anti-inflammatory at low doses (40mg daily), antibiotic at higher doses. Avoid in pregnancy, children <8 years, photosensitivity warning
MGD is the #1 cause of evaporative dry eye
Rosacea-associated blepharitis: look for facial telangiectasia, rhinophyma — treat the skin disease too (metronidazole gel)
Severe posterior blepharitis can cause corneal complications: phlyctenules, marginal infiltrates, corneal vascularization
pitfalls: Telling patients blepharitis is 'curable' — it is a chronic condition requiring ongoing management
Prescribing doxycycline to pregnant women or children under 8
Not looking for Demodex in refractory cases
Forgetting to treat associated dry eye disease

quizQuestions.question: What is the hallmark finding of Demodex blepharitis?
quizQuestions.answer: Cylindrical dandruff (collarettes) at the base of eyelashes

quizQuestions.question: Why should doxycycline NOT be used in children under 8?
quizQuestions.answer: Risk of permanent tooth discoloration and dental enamel hypoplasia

ppps.title: Dry Eye Syndrome

keyRecommendations.text: Classify dry eye as aqueous-deficient, evaporative, or mixed
Stepwise treatment approach: education/environmental modifications → artificial tears → anti-inflammatory therapy → advanced options
Topical cyclosporine (Restasis) or lifitegrast (Xiidra) for moderate-severe inflammatory dry eye
Punctal plugs for aqueous-deficient dry eye after controlling inflammation
Screen for systemic causes: Sjogren syndrome, rheumatoid arthritis, medications

diagnosticCriteria: Symptoms: burning, foreign body sensation, tearing (reflex), fluctuating vision
Signs: reduced TBUT (<10 seconds), reduced Schirmer test (<5mm/5min), corneal/conjunctival staining (fluorescein, lissamine green)
Meibomian gland dysfunction: thickened secretions, gland dropout on meibography
Sjogren syndrome: dry eyes + dry mouth + positive autoantibodies (anti-SSA/Ro, anti-SSB/La)

managementAlgorithm.action: Education: environmental modifications (humidifier, avoid fans/heaters), screen breaks (20-20-20 rule), omega-3 supplements
Artificial tears: preservative-free tears QID or more frequently; ointment at bedtime for severe cases
Anti-inflammatory: topical cyclosporine 0.05% BID or lifitegrast 5% BID; short course of topical steroid to bridge
Punctal occlusion: after controlling inflammation; start with temporary plugs, then permanent if effective
Advanced: autologous serum tears, amniotic membrane, scleral contact lenses, salivary gland transplantation
MGD-specific: warm compresses, LipiFlow, IPL, oral doxycycline

ppps.followUp: 4-8 weeks after starting anti-inflammatory therapy. Then every 3-6 months. Chronic management required.
boardPearls: Dry eye is the most common reason for ophthalmology visits
Evaporative dry eye (MGD) is more common than aqueous-deficient
Sjogren syndrome workup: anti-SSA (Ro), anti-SSB (La), ANA, RF, lip biopsy
Cyclosporine takes 3-6 months for full effect — bridge with short course of topical steroids
Do NOT place punctal plugs in actively inflamed eyes — will trap inflammatory mediators
Medications that worsen dry eye: antihistamines, decongestants, antidepressants, beta-blockers, isotretinoin
pitfalls: Placing punctal plugs before controlling inflammation — traps inflammatory cytokines
Not asking about medications that worsen dry eye
Not screening for Sjogren syndrome in appropriate patients
Using preserved artificial tears more than QID — preservatives cause toxicity

quizQuestions.question: Why should punctal plugs NOT be placed in actively inflamed dry eyes?
quizQuestions.answer: They trap inflammatory mediators on the ocular surface, potentially worsening the condition
distractors: They dissolve too quickly in inflamed tissue

quizQuestions.question: What autoantibodies are tested in suspected Sjogren syndrome?
quizQuestions.answer: Anti-SSA (Ro) and anti-SSB (La)

ppps.title: Conjunctivitis

keyRecommendations.text: Most conjunctivitis is viral — antibiotics are NOT routinely needed
Bacterial conjunctivitis: topical antibiotics (fluoroquinolone or erythromycin) for 5-7 days
Hyperacute conjunctivitis (severe purulent discharge): MUST rule out gonococcal — culture and Gram stain urgently
Allergic conjunctivitis: topical antihistamine/mast cell stabilizers (olopatadine), cool compresses
Contact lens wearers with red eye: discontinue lenses, treat as possible microbial keratitis until proven otherwise

diagnosticCriteria: Viral: watery discharge, follicles, preauricular lymphadenopathy, often bilateral, recent URI
Bacterial: mucopurulent discharge, papillae, morning crusting, often unilateral initially
Allergic: itching (hallmark), watery/mucoid discharge, papillae, bilateral, seasonal pattern
Hyperacute (gonococcal): copious purulent discharge, lid swelling, rapid onset — emergency

managementAlgorithm.action: Determine type: viral vs bacterial vs allergic based on history and exam
Viral: supportive (cool compresses, artificial tears, hand hygiene), contagious for 10-14 days
Bacterial: topical antibiotic (erythromycin ointment, fluoroquinolone drops) for 5-7 days
Hyperacute (suspected GC): Gram stain + culture urgently, systemic ceftriaxone 1g IM + topical erythromycin, daily lavage
Allergic: topical antihistamine/mast cell stabilizer (olopatadine BID), cool compresses, avoid allergens
Chlamydial: oral azithromycin 1g single dose + topical erythromycin; treat sexual partners

ppps.followUp: Viral: 2-3 weeks for resolution. Bacterial: if no improvement in 48-72 hours, reculture. Hyperacute: daily until improving. Allergic: seasonal management plan.
boardPearls: Itching = allergic conjunctivitis; purulent discharge = bacterial; watery + preauricular node = viral
Gonococcal conjunctivitis can perforate the cornea within 24-48 hours — this is an emergency
Adenovirus is the most common cause of viral conjunctivitis — highly contagious
EKC (epidemic keratoconjunctivitis): subepithelial infiltrates can persist for months — treat with low-dose topical steroid if visually significant
Chlamydial conjunctivitis: chronic follicular conjunctivitis in young adults — always treat systemically + treat partners
pitfalls: Prescribing antibiotics for viral conjunctivitis — this is unnecessary and contributes to resistance
Missing gonococcal conjunctivitis — rapid corneal perforation risk
Not treating sexual partners in chlamydial conjunctivitis
Using topical steroids in HSV keratitis masquerading as conjunctivitis

quizQuestions.question: What is the hallmark symptom that distinguishes allergic from other types of conjunctivitis?
quizQuestions.answer: Itching (pruritus)

quizQuestions.question: What is the treatment for suspected gonococcal conjunctivitis?
quizQuestions.answer: Systemic ceftriaxone 1g IM + topical erythromycin + cultures

ppps.title: Corneal Ectasia (Keratoconus)

keyRecommendations.text: Corneal cross-linking (CXL) is recommended for progressive keratoconus to halt progression
Contact lens fitting (rigid gas-permeable, scleral) is the mainstay of visual rehabilitation
Monitor for progression with serial corneal topography
Corneal transplantation (DALK or PK) for advanced cases not manageable with contact lenses

diagnosticCriteria: Corneal thinning and protrusion (usually inferotemporal)
Topography: inferior steepening, asymmetric bowtie pattern, skewed radial axes
Signs: Vogt striae, Fleischer ring, Munson sign (V-shaped indentation of lower lid on downgaze)
Tomography (Pentacam): thin pachymetry, elevated posterior float, abnormal indices

managementAlgorithm.action: Diagnose and stage severity: mild, moderate, advanced keratoconus based on K values, pachymetry, visual function
If progressive: corneal cross-linking (CXL) — riboflavin + UVA, minimum corneal thickness 400 microns
Visual rehabilitation: glasses (mild), RGP contact lenses (moderate), scleral lenses (advanced)
Intracorneal ring segments (ICRS/Intacs) for patients intolerant of contact lenses with clear central cornea
Corneal transplant: DALK (deep anterior lamellar keratoplasty) preferred over PK when possible — lower rejection risk
Acute hydrops: topical hypertonic saline, cycloplegia, NOT immediate surgery — wait for edema to resolve

ppps.followUp: Every 6-12 months with serial topography/tomography for progression monitoring. More frequently if young patient or recent CXL.
boardPearls: CXL stops progression in >95% of cases — this is a game-changer for young patients
Minimum corneal thickness for standard CXL: 400 microns (risk of endothelial damage if thinner)
DALK preserves host endothelium — lower rejection risk than PK
Acute hydrops = Descemet membrane rupture — treat conservatively, NOT with immediate surgery
Keratoconus associations: Down syndrome, atopy, eye rubbing, Leber congenital amaurosis
Munson sign, Vogt striae, Fleischer ring — know these classic signs
pitfalls: Operating on acute hydrops — wait for edema to resolve (weeks to months)
Not screening for keratoconus before refractive surgery — LASIK on keratoconus = ectasia
Performing CXL on corneas <400 microns without hypoosmolar riboflavin protocol
Not counseling patients to stop eye rubbing — this accelerates progression

quizQuestions.question: What is the minimum corneal thickness required for standard corneal cross-linking?
quizQuestions.answer: 400 microns

quizQuestions.question: What is the correct management of acute hydrops in keratoconus?
quizQuestions.answer: Conservative treatment (hypertonic saline, cycloplegia) — NOT immediate surgery

ppps.title: Amblyopia

keyRecommendations.text: Amblyopia screening should begin in infancy and at every well-child visit
Full cycloplegic refraction is essential before treatment — correct refractive error first
Patching 2 hours/day is as effective as 6 hours/day for moderate amblyopia (PEDIG)
Atropine penalization (1% to sound eye) is an effective alternative to patching
Treatment can be effective up to age 17, but earlier treatment yields better outcomes

diagnosticCriteria: Reduced best-corrected VA in one or both eyes without structural abnormality
Types: strabismic, refractive (anisometropic or isoametropic), deprivation (most severe)
At least 2-line interocular difference or VA below age-expected norms

managementAlgorithm.action: Full cycloplegic refraction (atropine 1% or cyclopentolate 1%) — correct refractive error first
Glasses trial for 16-18 weeks before adding patching — refractive correction alone may resolve amblyopia
Moderate amblyopia (20/40 to 20/100): patch better eye 2 hours/day OR atropine 1% to better eye on weekends
Severe amblyopia (20/100 to 20/400): patch better eye 6 hours/day
Continue treatment until VA stabilizes (no improvement over 2-3 consecutive visits)
Maintenance/weaning: taper patching gradually to prevent recurrence

ppps.followUp: Every 6-12 weeks during active treatment. Then every 3-6 months during maintenance. Monitor until visual maturity (~age 10).
boardPearls: PEDIG ATS: 2 hours/day patching is as effective as 6 hours/day for moderate amblyopia
Atropine penalization: weekend-only atropine can be effective for moderate amblyopia
Refractive correction alone resolves amblyopia in ~30% of cases — always try glasses first
Deprivation amblyopia is the MOST SEVERE form — congenital cataracts must be removed within 6-8 weeks
Treatment beyond the 'critical period': PEDIG showed benefit up to age 17, challenging old dogma
Recurrence rate is ~25% after stopping treatment — taper gradually
pitfalls: Starting patching before giving adequate glasses trial (16-18 weeks)
Patching too aggressively in young children — can cause reverse amblyopia in the patched eye
Telling parents 'it is too late' for treatment in older children — benefit shown up to age 17
Not treating the underlying cause (strabismus, cataract) in addition to the amblyopia

quizQuestions.question: Per PEDIG, how many hours of patching is needed for moderate amblyopia?
quizQuestions.answer: 2 hours per day (shown to be as effective as 6 hours)

quizQuestions.question: What should be done BEFORE starting patching for amblyopia?
quizQuestions.answer: Full cycloplegic refraction and adequate trial of glasses alone (16-18 weeks)

ppps.title: Esotropia and Exotropia

keyRecommendations.text: Full cycloplegic refraction is the FIRST step for any child with esotropia
Accommodative esotropia: full hyperopic correction is the treatment — surgery only for non-accommodative residual
Infantile esotropia: surgical correction ideally between 6-24 months for best binocular outcome
Intermittent exotropia: observe if well-controlled, surgery if decompensating
Treat amblyopia before or concurrent with strabismus surgery

diagnosticCriteria: Infantile esotropia: onset <6 months, large-angle (>30 PD), cross-fixation pattern
Accommodative esotropia: onset 6 months to 7 years, hyperopia ≥+2.00, improves with hyperopic correction
Intermittent exotropia: intermittent deviation, often worse at distance, closing one eye in sunlight
Measure: cover-uncover test, alternate cover + prism bar for quantification, at distance and near

managementAlgorithm.action: Full cycloplegic refraction (atropine 1% for children <1 year, cyclopentolate for older)
Accommodative ET: prescribe full hyperopic correction, reassess in 6-8 weeks
High AC/A ratio (near deviation >> distance): bifocal add or progressive lenses
Non-accommodative residual (>8-10 PD with full correction): surgical correction
Infantile ET: bilateral medial rectus recession (most common procedure)
Intermittent XT: observe if well-controlled, surgery when decompensating or >50% of time exotropic

ppps.followUp: Post-glasses: 6-8 weeks. Post-surgery: 1 day, 1 week, 1 month, then every 3-6 months. Long-term monitoring for recurrence.
boardPearls: Full hyperopic correction is the TREATMENT for accommodative esotropia — do NOT under-correct
High AC/A ratio: near deviation exceeds distance deviation by ≥10 PD — add bifocal
Infantile esotropia: DVD (dissociated vertical deviation) and IOOA commonly associated
Bilateral medial rectus recession is the most common surgery for infantile esotropia
Cover test at distance AND near is essential — different patterns guide treatment
Sensory esotropia: large-angle ET secondary to unilateral poor vision — address underlying cause first
pitfalls: Under-correcting hyperopia — prescribe the FULL cycloplegic refraction for accommodative ET
Doing strabismus surgery before adequate trial of glasses
Not treating amblyopia before or concurrent with surgery
Missing a cranial nerve palsy presenting as new-onset strabismus in an adult

quizQuestions.question: What is the FIRST step in managing a child with esotropia?
quizQuestions.answer: Full cycloplegic refraction to check for hyperopia
distractors: Botox injection to the medial rectus

quizQuestions.question: What indicates a high AC/A ratio in esotropia?
quizQuestions.answer: Near deviation exceeds distance deviation by ≥10 prism diopters

ppps.title: Pediatric Eye Evaluations

keyRecommendations.text: Red reflex test at every well-baby visit — absent or asymmetric reflex requires urgent referral
Vision screening at age 3-5 using age-appropriate optotypes (LEA symbols, HOTV)
Photoscreening or autorefraction-based devices acceptable for screening ages 1-5
Leukocoria: ALWAYS rule out retinoblastoma — urgent ophthalmology referral

diagnosticCriteria: Abnormal red reflex: absent, white (leukocoria), asymmetric — immediate referral
Failed vision screening: unable to read 20/40 at age 3-4, 20/30 at age 5+, or 2-line interocular difference
Strabismus detected: any manifest deviation or concern from parents

managementAlgorithm.action: Newborn: red reflex test, external exam, pupil response
6 months: fix and follow, check for strabismus, red reflex
3-5 years: visual acuity screening with age-appropriate optotypes
Any abnormality: comprehensive ophthalmologic examination
Leukocoria: urgent referral — retinoblastoma until proven otherwise

ppps.followUp: Per AAP screening schedule at well-child visits. Comprehensive eye exam by age 5 for all children.
boardPearls: Red reflex test is the single most important screening test for pediatric eye disease
Leukocoria differential: retinoblastoma, PHPV/PFV, Coats disease, cataract, ROP, toxocariasis
Retinoblastoma: NEVER biopsy intraocularly — tumor seeding risk
Fix and follow: both eyes should fixate and maintain fixation through a blink — poor fix/follow suggests amblyopia
Photoscreening can detect risk factors for amblyopia (high refractive error, anisometropia, media opacity, strabismus)
pitfalls: Dismissing an abnormal red reflex — ALWAYS investigate further
Not performing red reflex test at every well-baby visit
Missing unilateral poor fixation in an infant — could indicate amblyopia or serious pathology
Assuming all leukocoria is benign — retinoblastoma must be ruled out first

quizQuestions.question: What is the single most important screening test for pediatric eye disease?
quizQuestions.answer: Red reflex test (Bruckner test)

quizQuestions.question: What is the most important diagnosis to rule out in a child with leukocoria?
quizQuestions.answer: Retinoblastoma

ppps.title: Refractive Errors & Presbyopia

keyRecommendations.text: Cycloplegic refraction is essential in children to reveal full hyperopic error
Manifest refraction should be performed with endpoint determination (least minus/most plus for best VA)
Contact lens fitting requires proper training on insertion/removal, hygiene, and follow-up schedules
Progressive myopia in children: consider atropine 0.01-0.05%, multifocal contact lenses, or orthokeratology

diagnosticCriteria: Myopia: distant objects blurred, near vision typically clear, minus lens correction
Hyperopia: may have clear distance vision (if accommodating), near difficulty, plus lens correction
Astigmatism: distorted/blurred vision at all distances, cylinder correction needed
Presbyopia: age-related loss of accommodation (onset ~40 years), difficulty with near tasks

managementAlgorithm.action: Determine refractive error: manifest refraction, cycloplegic if indicated (children, hyperopes)
Prescribe appropriate correction: spectacles, contact lenses, or refractive surgery discussion
Myopia control for progressive myopia in children: low-dose atropine, multifocal CLs, or orthoK
Presbyopia: reading glasses, bifocals, progressive addition lenses, multifocal CLs, or monovision
Contact lens wearers: annual exam with corneal evaluation, discuss risks of overnight wear

ppps.followUp: Annual comprehensive eye exam. Contact lens wearers: annual with corneal evaluation. Myopia control: every 6 months.
boardPearls: Cycloplegic refraction in children reveals latent hyperopia — always use in strabismus workup
Low-dose atropine (0.01-0.05%) reduces myopia progression by ~50% — becoming standard of care
Orthokeratology: overnight corneal reshaping lens for myopia control — effective but requires careful monitoring
Progressive myopia risk factors: younger age of onset, Asian ethnicity, two myopic parents, limited outdoor time
Contact lens complications: microbial keratitis (1 in 500/year for daily wear, 1 in 80 for extended wear)
pitfalls: Not doing cycloplegic refraction in children — will underprescribe hyperopic correction
Prescribing too much minus (over-minusing) — always use least minus for best VA
Not discussing myopia control options with parents of progressive myopia children
Prescribing extended-wear contact lenses without discussing increased keratitis risk

quizQuestions.question: What is the approximate risk reduction in myopia progression with low-dose atropine?
quizQuestions.answer: ~50% reduction in progression

quizQuestions.question: Why is cycloplegic refraction essential in children with esotropia?
quizQuestions.answer: To reveal the full hyperopic error (latent hyperopia) which may be causing accommodative esotropia
distractors: To dilate the pupil for fundus exam

ppps.title: Refractive Surgery (LASIK/PRK)

keyRecommendations.text: Screen for keratoconus and forme fruste keratoconus before any corneal refractive surgery
Stable refraction for at least 1 year before surgery
Adequate corneal thickness: residual stromal bed ≥250 microns after LASIK
Informed consent must include risks: dry eye, halos/glare, under/overcorrection, ectasia, flap complications
PRK preferred over LASIK for thin corneas, anterior basement membrane dystrophy, or contact sports

diagnosticCriteria: Candidate for LASIK: stable refraction, adequate corneal thickness, no keratoconus, no autoimmune disease, realistic expectations
Contraindications: keratoconus/ectasia, unstable refraction, pregnancy, autoimmune disease, severe dry eye
SMILE: small incision lenticule extraction — alternative to LASIK with smaller incision

managementAlgorithm.action: Screening: topography/tomography (rule out keratoconus), pachymetry, dry eye evaluation, pupil size, manifest AND cycloplegic refraction
Determine procedure: LASIK (most common), PRK (thin corneas, ABMD), SMILE, or ICL (high myopia)
Ensure residual stromal bed ≥250 microns (LASIK) — calculate: pachymetry - flap thickness - ablation depth
Post-op: topical steroid taper + antibiotic (LASIK); longer steroid course for PRK (risk of haze)
Complications: dry eye (common, usually temporary), flap complications (wrinkles, displacement), ectasia (rare but serious), haze (PRK)

ppps.followUp: LASIK: 1 day, 1 week, 1 month, 3 months, 6 months, 1 year. PRK: more frequent early due to longer recovery. Dry eye management ongoing.
boardPearls: Residual stromal bed ≥250 microns is the critical safety threshold for LASIK
Post-LASIK ectasia: the most feared complication — caused by insufficient residual stroma or undetected keratoconus
LASIK on keratoconus = ectasia — this is why screening topography is mandatory
PRK advantages over LASIK: no flap complications, can treat thinner corneas, better for contact sports
IOL calculation after refractive surgery is challenging — need adjusted K values (Barrett True-K, clinical history method)
Epithelial ingrowth after LASIK: monitor for progression, may need flap lift and debridement
pitfalls: Performing LASIK without topography screening for keratoconus
Not calculating residual stromal bed before LASIK
Not documenting pre-LASIK K values — needed for future IOL calculations
Performing LASIK during pregnancy (refraction unstable)
Not discussing realistic expectations and potential need for enhancement

quizQuestions.question: What is the minimum residual stromal bed thickness after LASIK?
quizQuestions.answer: 250 microns

quizQuestions.question: What is the most feared complication of LASIK?
quizQuestions.answer: Post-LASIK ectasia (corneal ectasia/keratectasia)

ppps.title: Cataract in the Adult Eye

keyRecommendations.text: Surgery is indicated when cataract causes functional visual impairment affecting daily activities
Phacoemulsification with posterior chamber IOL implantation is the standard of care
Biometry (IOL Master or immersion A-scan) with modern formulas for IOL calculation
Discuss IOL options: monofocal, toric, multifocal, extended depth of focus (EDOF)
Topical NSAID + steroid post-operatively to prevent inflammation and CME

diagnosticCriteria: Decreased visual acuity attributable to lens opacity
Functional impairment: difficulty driving, reading, or performing daily activities
Types: nuclear sclerotic, cortical, posterior subcapsular (PSC)
PSC cataracts: associated with steroid use, diabetes, radiation, uveitis

managementAlgorithm.action: Confirm cataract as cause of visual symptoms — rule out other pathology (AMD, glaucoma, DR)
Biometry: IOL Master (preferred) or immersion A-scan; use multiple formulas (Barrett Universal II, SRK/T, Haigis)
Pre-op: treat ocular surface disease, manage IOP if glaucoma, optimize diabetic control
Surgery: phacoemulsification + PCIOL in the capsular bag
Post-op: topical steroid (prednisolone 1% taper) + NSAID (ketorolac or nepafenac) + antibiotic x 4 weeks
PCO (posterior capsular opacification): Nd:YAG laser capsulotomy when visually significant

ppps.followUp: 1 day, 1 week, 1 month post-op. Refraction at 4-6 weeks. Final glasses prescription when refraction stable.
boardPearls: Barrett Universal II formula is gaining favor for all axial lengths
Post-refractive surgery IOL calculation: must use adjusted K values — clinical history method, Barrett True-K, or Haigis-L
Posterior capsular rupture management: anterior vitrectomy, IOL in sulcus (reduce power 0.5-1.0 D), consider optic capture
IFIS (Intraoperative Floppy Iris Syndrome): caused by tamsulosin — use iris hooks or Malyugin ring
Toxic anterior segment syndrome (TASS): sterile inflammation 12-48 hours post-op — treat aggressively with hourly steroids
Endophthalmitis post-cataract: 1 in 1000-3000; most common organism Staph epidermidis (acute) or P. acnes (chronic)
Nd:YAG capsulotomy: wait at least 3-6 months after cataract surgery; risks include RD, CME, IOL pitting
pitfalls: Operating on a cataract when another condition (AMD, glaucoma) is the primary cause of vision loss
Not asking about tamsulosin (Flomax) preoperatively — IFIS risk
Using the wrong IOL formula for axial length extremes
Not documenting pre-operative K values in refractive surgery patients
Confusing TASS (early, sterile) with endophthalmitis (later, infectious)

quizQuestions.question: What medication causes IFIS (Intraoperative Floppy Iris Syndrome)?
quizQuestions.answer: Tamsulosin (Flomax) — an alpha-1 blocker

quizQuestions.question: How do you adjust IOL power when placing in the sulcus instead of the bag?
quizQuestions.answer: Reduce power by 0.5 to 1.0 diopter

ppps.title: Comprehensive Adult Medical Eye Evaluation

keyRecommendations.text: Asymptomatic adults aged 40-54 with no risk factors: comprehensive exam every 2-4 years
Ages 55-64: every 1-3 years
Ages 65+: every 1-2 years
Diabetic patients: annual dilated exam (type 2 at diagnosis, type 1 within 5 years)
African Americans: increased risk of glaucoma — screening from age 40

diagnosticCriteria: Complete exam includes: history, VA, pupils, EOM, confrontation fields, IOP, slit lamp, dilated fundus exam
Document: refraction, gonioscopy when indicated, optic nerve assessment

managementAlgorithm.action: Complete history: ocular, medical, family, medications, allergies, social history
Visual acuity with correction, pupils (RAPD), IOP measurement
Anterior segment examination: lids, conjunctiva, cornea, AC, iris, lens
Dilated posterior segment: optic nerve, macula, vessels, peripheral retina
Ancillary testing as indicated: OCT, visual fields, pachymetry, gonioscopy
Assessment, plan, and appropriate follow-up interval

ppps.followUp: Based on age and risk factors as per screening guidelines above.
boardPearls: Comprehensive eye exam is the foundation of all ophthalmology — know the components
Every exam should include pupil check (RAPD), IOP, and at minimum a peripheral lens exam
Know the screening intervals by age group and risk factors — this is commonly tested
African Americans and Hispanics have higher glaucoma prevalence — earlier screening recommended
pitfalls: Skipping pupil exam — RAPD is the most valuable single clinical test in ophthalmology
Not dilating patients who need it — fundus pathology can be missed
Not asking about medication history — many medications affect the eyes

quizQuestions.question: How often should asymptomatic adults aged 65+ have a comprehensive eye exam?
quizQuestions.answer: Every 1-2 years

quizQuestions.question: When should type 2 diabetic patients first have a dilated eye exam?
quizQuestions.answer: At the time of diabetes diagnosis

ppps.title: Hydroxychloroquine Retinopathy Screening

keyRecommendations.text: Baseline eye exam within the first year of starting hydroxychloroquine
Annual screening after 5 years of use (or sooner if risk factors present)
Primary screening tests: SD-OCT and automated visual fields (10-2)
Recommended dose: ≤5 mg/kg real body weight/day to minimize toxicity risk
Risk factors for toxicity: dose >5 mg/kg/day, cumulative dose, renal impairment, concomitant tamoxifen use

diagnosticCriteria: Early toxicity: parafoveal thinning on OCT, paracentral scotomas on 10-2 visual field
Advanced toxicity: bulls-eye maculopathy on fundoscopy, severely depressed visual fields
mfERG: paracentral depression (more sensitive than visual fields for early detection)

managementAlgorithm.action: Baseline exam: VA, dilated fundus exam, SD-OCT macula, 10-2 visual fields
Low-risk patients: annual screening after 5 years of use
High-risk patients (dose >5mg/kg, renal disease, >10 years of use): annual screening from the start
If toxicity detected: STOP the drug immediately and refer to rheumatologist for alternative
Toxicity is IRREVERSIBLE and can progress even after stopping the drug — early detection is critical

ppps.followUp: Annual after 5 years of use. Immediately if any screening test is abnormal.
boardPearls: Updated AAO guidelines (2016/2026): real body weight dosing at ≤5 mg/kg/day
SD-OCT showing parafoveal thinning is often the earliest detectable sign
Bulls-eye maculopathy is a LATE sign — by this point, damage is extensive and irreversible
Asian patients may show pericentral pattern (outside the parafoveal zone) — can be missed on 10-2 fields
Risk increases significantly after 5 years of use and with cumulative dose >1000g
Toxicity can PROGRESS even after stopping the drug — there is no treatment
pitfalls: Using ideal body weight instead of real body weight for dosing calculation
Not screening Asian patients with wider visual field patterns (they may have pericentral toxicity pattern)
Waiting for bulls-eye maculopathy to diagnose — this is too late
Not communicating findings to the prescribing rheumatologist promptly

quizQuestions.question: What is the maximum recommended dose of hydroxychloroquine to minimize retinal toxicity?
quizQuestions.answer: ≤5 mg/kg real body weight per day

quizQuestions.question: What is the primary screening test for HCQ toxicity per AAO guidelines?
quizQuestions.answer: SD-OCT and automated visual fields (10-2)
